primary_diagnosis.name: Linear IgA bullous dermatosis, drug-induced (mild, localised)

pathophysiology_summary: Linear IgA bullous dermatosis is an autoimmune subepidermal blistering disease in which IgA autoantibodies deposit in a linear pattern along the basement-membrane zone, producing tense subepidermal vesicles that classically cluster in annular 'string of pearls' arrangements. A common trigger is drug exposure (vancomycin is the archetype, but many antibiotics are implicated), with onset typically within days of starting the offending drug. The drug-induced form is usually self-limiting and resolves once the offending agent is withdrawn. The diagnosis is confirmed by skin biopsy with direct immunofluorescence showing linear IgA at the dermo-epidermal junction — an investigation that cannot be obtained via telehealth. The clinical importance of this presentation is not the LABD itself, which is mild here, but that an acute blistering eruption after a new drug shares its opening picture with Stevens-Johnson syndrome / toxic epidermal necrolysis (a life-threatening severe cutaneous adverse reaction) and can be confused with anaphylaxis; those must be excluded, and the exam that reliably excludes them (mucosal survey, Nikolsky sign) is a bedside procedure telehealth cannot perform.

differential.diagnosis: drug-induced blistering eruption (Linear IgA bullous dermatosis)
differential.evidence_basis: Acute blistering eruption within days of a new antibiotic; tense clear vesicles in annular clusters localised to the forearms.
A blistering eruption after a new drug is the opening picture of SJS/TEN — a life-threatening severe cutaneous adverse reaction that must be actively excluded before reassurance.
New drug plus an acute skin reaction raises anaphylaxis, which must be screened for even though blisters are not a typical anaphylaxis feature.
A blistering skin eruption; less likely given clear non-purulent serous fluid and absence of honey-coloured crusting.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the AI Doctor should hold the drug-reaction picture as leading BUT keep SJS/TEN and anaphylaxis explicitly on the list and screen them; failing to raise the severe mimics is the key reasoning error.

differential.diagnosis: drug-induced Linear IgA bullous dermatosis (mild, localised)
differential.evidence_basis: No mucosal involvement, no systemic upset, no skin sloughing, localised distribution — the picture is a mild localised drug-induced blistering eruption.

History screen is reassuring (no mucosal sores, no sloughing, systemically well), but SJS/TEN cannot be definitively excluded by telehealth — the mucosal exam and Nikolsky sign require an in-person assessment.
No airway, breathing or circulatory involvement, no urticaria or angioedema — anaphylaxis is excluded on history.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the screen, LABD is leading and anaphylaxis falls away, but SJS/TEN must remain an important-not-to-miss that drives an urgent in-person assessment, because the confirming/excluding examination cannot be done remotely.

differential.diagnosis: drug-induced Linear IgA bullous dermatosis (mild, localised) — presumptive, pending biopsy and direct immunofluorescence
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed in the source by in-person exam (negative Nikolsky, clear mucosa, annular tense vesicles) and to be verified by skin biopsy with direct immunofluorescence; drug withdrawal is expected to resolve it.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Definitive diagnosis needs biopsy + immunofluorescence; via telehealth the correct end state is a presumptive drug-reaction diagnosis with SJS/TEN excluded only by urgent in-person assessment.

red_flags.description: Mucosal involvement (mouth, eye or genital sores/erosions) — the hallmark of Stevens-Johnson syndrome / TEN
red_flags.clinical_significance: Mucosal erosions distinguish life-threatening SJS/TEN from a mild localised bullous drug eruption; their presence mandates emergency care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing early mucosal SJS/TEN risks progression to extensive epidermal detachment, sepsis and death.

red_flags.description: Airway/breathing involvement — throat tightness, wheeze, lip/tongue/facial swelling (anaphylaxis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals anaphylaxis/airway compromise requiring immediate adrenaline and 000.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unrecognised airway involvement can progress to fatal obstruction.

red_flags.description: Systemic toxicity — high fever, malaise (prodrome of severe cutaneous adverse reaction)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Fever and malaise before or with a blistering rash suggest a severe cutaneous adverse reaction rather than a benign localised eruption.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Reassuring a patient who is developing systemic SCAR delays life-saving hospital care.

red_flags.description: Skin detachment in sheets / positive Nikolsky sign / rapidly spreading blistering (TEN)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Sloughing of the epidermis in sheets is the defining feature of toxic epidermal necrolysis; a positive Nikolsky sign requires in-person elicitation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed epidermal detachment is a dermatological emergency with high mortality if not admitted.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Labelling an acute post-antibiotic blistering eruption a 'mild allergy' and reassuring the patient without screening for, or arranging exclusion of, SJS/TEN — the single most dangerous error in this case.

Believing the diagnosis can be made or SJS excluded over chat. The Nikolsky sign and mucosal survey that safely excluded SJS in the source were an in-person examination; telehealth cannot reproduce them, so an urgent in-person assessment is required.
Fixating on 'reaction to a new drug' and treating a stable, breathing-normally patient with no airway signs as anaphylaxis — calling 000 for a mild localised eruption is over-triage.
Not instructing immediate cessation of the offending antibiotic, or worse, reassuring the patient to complete the course — continued exposure drives the reaction.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Nikolsky sign (whether the epidermis shears with lateral pressure)
Definitive mucosal status on examination (oral, conjunctival, genital)
Whether the vesicles are truly tense subepidermal blisters and their exact morphology/pattern
Objective vital signs (blood pressure, heart rate, temperature, oxygen saturation) without a home device
Skin biopsy with direct immunofluorescence (linear IgA at the basement-membrane zone) — the confirmatory test
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Instruct immediate cessation of the recently started antibiotic; screen by history for mucosal, ocular, airway, systemic and skin-sloughing red flags; and arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment because the examination that excludes SJS/TEN cannot be performed over chat. Provide explicit safety-netting for the red flags that mandate emergency care.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: High risk of falsely reassuring the eruption as a benign allergy and missing early SJS/TEN, which telehealth cannot exclude by examination; a smaller opposite risk is over-calling anaphylaxis and sending a stable patient to 000.
